blocks.text: **Hardware** refers to the physical components of a computer — the parts you can actually touch. Every piece of hardware has a specific role in the system, and they all work together orchestrated by the CPU.
The CPU — Brain of the Computer
The **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** executes program instructions. It performs arithmetic, logic, and control operations billions of times per second.

rows: Performs math (+, -, ×, ÷) and logical comparisons (AND, OR, NOT)
Fetches and decodes instructions, directs other components
Tiny ultra-fast storage inside the CPU for data being actively used
Fast memory built into the CPU to reduce RAM access time

blocks.title: CPU Speed
blocks.text: CPU speed is measured in **GHz (gigahertz)** — a 3.5 GHz CPU can execute 3.5 billion cycles per second. Modern CPUs have **multiple cores**, allowing them to run several tasks simultaneously.
Computers have two fundamentally different types of memory, each with a distinct purpose:

tabs.label: RAM (Primary Memory)
tabs.content: **RAM (Random Access Memory)** is temporary, fast memory used while the computer is running. When you open a browser, its data loads into RAM. When you shut down, RAM is wiped completely. Modern computers typically have 8–64 GB of RAM.

tabs.label: SSD / HDD (Secondary Storage)
tabs.content: **Solid State Drives (SSD)** and **Hard Disk Drives (HDD)** provide permanent storage. Your files, OS, and applications live here even when power is off. SSDs are much faster than HDDs — typically 10–100× — but were historically more expensive.

tabs.label: Cache Memory
tabs.content: **Cache** is a small amount of extremely fast memory built directly into the CPU. The processor checks cache before RAM. **L1 cache** (fastest, ~32–256KB per core), **L2** (~256KB–4MB), **L3** (shared, ~4–64MB). The hierarchy trades speed for size.

blocks.text: The **motherboard** is the main circuit board that connects all components. It hosts the CPU socket, RAM slots, PCIe slots (for GPU and other cards), SATA ports (for drives), and the BIOS/UEFI chip.

blocks.title: The Bus
blocks.text: Components communicate over **buses** — electrical pathways on the motherboard. The **front-side bus** connects CPU to RAM; **PCIe** connects CPU to GPU and other expansion cards. Bus width (in bits) and speed determine data transfer rates.

A **GPU** was originally designed to render graphics quickly. It has thousands of small cores designed to process many operations in parallel — which makes GPUs ideal not just for gaming, but for **machine learning**, scientific simulation, and video encoding.

items.label: Input
items.desc: Keyboard, mouse, touchscreen, webcam, microphone, scanner, sensors

items.label: Both (I/O)
items.desc: Touchscreen, network interface card (NIC), USB devices

points: The CPU contains an ALU (math/logic), Control Unit, registers, and cache
RAM is fast but temporary — it's lost when power is removed
SSDs are faster than HDDs; cache is faster than both but much smaller
The motherboard is the backbone that connects all components via buses
GPUs excel at parallel computation — originally for graphics, now also for AI

questions.question: Which type of memory loses all its data when the computer is turned off?
questions.explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile — it only holds data while powered. When you shut down, everything in RAM is lost.

questions.question: Why are GPUs used for machine learning and AI training?
options: They have a higher clock speed than CPUs
They have thousands of cores suited for parallel computation
They have more RAM than CPUs
questions.explanation: GPUs have thousands of small cores that can process many operations simultaneously. Neural network training involves massive parallel matrix operations — exactly what GPUs excel at.
